sentences.text: As I had said earlier, life was fairly comfortable in Westerbork.
You have to remember that I never knew anything other than camp life.
I was four years old when we left Germany, and almost immediately, were incarcerated, almost immediate, were in some sort of a camp situation.
And for me, and I believe also for my brother, it was quite comfortable being in Westerbork.
I made friends.
There were many, many children.
And we had some sort of education.
It was quite primitive.

But groups would get together with teachers who would teach us preliminary lessons-- arithmetic, German at that time-- even though we were in Holland, it was German that we were taught, because we were with German-speaking people.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we-- we, as children, didn't feel the discomfort nearly as did the adults.
And we didn't feel as threatened as the adults, because, as I said, we never knew anything different.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As far as toys and games, there was nothing.
I did have one little rag doll that I always had with me.
We made up our own games.
I remember that I [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] made believe-- I always want a little puppy or something of my own.
And I remember, in the summer time, or when the sun would shine, I had a little mirror with me.
And with the reflection of the mirror on the ground would be my puppy and my pets.
And I would walk along, and always looking back at him and making believe that he was real.
And we also had-- would save certain pieces of-- of wrapping paper that we would find or we would get.
And we would smoothen it with our nails, and we would save that.
And we would trade the papers.
The-- the prettier the-- the wrap, the more valuable.
And sometimes, you would trade a-- a shiny gold or silver one for two little plainer ones.
And we had our own little games that we played.
Instead of playing with marbles, we had little stones.
And we used to play with those.
And we did play hopscotch.
And we passed-- the time passed by.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] life was all right.
I do remember one terrible incident.
I was a very poor eater.
I mean, as I said, food was all right there, but I was a poor eater.
And I always had sore throats.
And it was decided that I had adenoids that had to be removed.
And yes, they were removed, but without anesthesia.
And I recuperated.
I remember getting cold ice cream or something afterwards.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I survived it.
And it was OK.
I guess the pain must have been very severe.
But I don't remember it.
And perhaps try-- I'm trying to suppress it.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I-- I do recall later on-- as I said, we were in-- in Westerbork really almost four years.
We were what was called the [GERMAN].
We were the first ones to be there.
So we were very much accustomed to life there.
And things did become a little bit more difficult later long, because so many more thousands and thousands of additional people came from throughout Europe.
And everything got very-- tightened quarters, and we, as I said, had to share our little quarter with another family.
But it-- it didn't matter to us children.
We never minded.
Adults were always saying that the transports that were leaving Westerbork for the-- there were weekly transports and-- that were leaving for the concentration camps.

That was always a terrible burden for the adults to bear, and-- because they did know that life in the camps in Eastern Europe were dreadful, and didn't know at that point that they-- that they were extermination camps.
But they did know that their lives would be endangered and that things were getting increasingly worse.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] As I said, for us children, life really was bearable.
It was even comfortable.
And-- and even with that, we were-- we welcomed to leave, only because we children did not know what was ahead of us.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that was about all in Westerbork that I-- I can remember.
And my mother worked hard in-- as a-- in the kitchen, always in the kitchen duty, and my father always with the shoes and always repairing shoes and ripping shoes apart.
And it was more busy work for them than anything else.
There were-- many very, very talented people came to Westerbork from various parts of Europe.
And there was a man by the name of Willy Rosen, who was a cabaret artist.
And I remember reading-- I was never allowed to go to these cabarets.
But some of the adults did go.
And of course, the cabaret was for-- mostly for the-- for the German Gestapo, for the SS.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when the people were to applaud, they were told, you don't applaud for Jews.
And-- but of course, it was a very professional production.
The-- the props were nothing.
But the talent was very professional.
And-- so there were all sorts of things going on in-- in Westerbork at that time.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] That's-- that's about all that I-- I can think of in Westerbork.
Westerbork was-- was surrounded by fields of-- of heather, I recall.
I'm sorry if so often I think there's nothing more to say, and then I come up with something else.
And the heather was of these very lovely shades of lavender and purple.
And there were fields of that.
And then there were flowers, [GERMAN].
I don't know what they're called in-- in-- in English.
But a lot of that, I-- I do remember-- not that we could touch the flowers.
They were on the outside of the barbed wire.
But there was some beauty in that camp.
And-- and I guess, as children, we looked for that.
And we looked to see the more positive things in life, because we-- we didn't have that fear.
And we didn't have the knowledge that the adults had.
And therefore, we just [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] took everything very, very light, as children normally do.
Well, the last few weeks, obviously, were the absolute worst.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were all suffering from malnutrition, dysentery, typhoid, filth, hunger, fear-- that constant fear of not knowing what's to come next.
And the hunger, actually, after a while wasn't so painful anymore.
Stomachs shrink after a while.
And you don't feel that hunger as-- as much.
I weighed 35 pounds, about 16, 17 kilos at the time when we left Bergen-Belsen.
That's, as I said, about 35 pounds.
And I was just 10 years old.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There are really no pictures.
There are no words that can describe that-- the constant foul odor, the horror, the-- the-- the fear, and the constant death that was surrounding us.
We would trip over the dead.
It was common to see people just fall and die, and-- or just not to wake up, or just to lay down because they-- they were weak and they couldn't go on anymore, and the next minute, they were gone.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] As I said, Bergen-Belsen was not an extermination camp.
There were crematorium.
We had the high chimneys.
To this day, when I see a chimney, I cringe.
I mean, they had to burn the dead.
There were so many.
But it was not an extermination camp, per se.
And the Germans-- we just-- the Germans couldn't get rid of us fast enough.

So it was decided-- towards April of '45-- to send us to what we thought would be Auschwitz, because Auschwitz, as it's-- as an extermination camp, would be the solution for the majority of us, or for all of us.
We were-- those who were able to walk were walked to the station again.
And we were put on a train.
This was a very long train.
There were some cattle cars, some so-called passenger cars.
I remember we were in an old passenger car.
I believe we were with my-- my father and my brother, my mother and I-- we were together.
We were on this train for two full weeks.
Actually, it was 13 days-- 13 days and 13 nights, and this without food and without water.
It is amazing.
It is miraculous how any one of us could have survived.
The train went very slowly.
It was-- ordinarily, it would have taken maybe nine to 10 hours to get to Auschwitz.
But because the Germans tried to evade the Allies, we were on this train for almost two weeks.
And the trains-- the train would come to a stop every so often.
We were allowed, those who were able to, get off and take a little water from a stream.
My mother would collect a little water from a locomotive.
And that's how we got our drink.
As far as hunger, I know that we-- we didn't feel that anymore.
But thirst and sleep was always needed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Drink was needed.
This train was heading, as I said, towards Eastern Europe.
And it was towards the end of our trip-- was-- that the Germans stormed through the train and demanded civilian clothing, our civilian clothing.
We then knew that the war was coming towards an end.
While on the train, there were constant bombings and constant planes and shootings and whatnot.
We never knew whether we would be hit.
And even if we did, so probably would have been just as well.
There came a time where we really did not care to go on anymore and didn't think that it-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that that-- that we would survive.
And yet, deep down, I personally always felt that things had to get just a little bit worse before they can get better.
And I always said, as long as we can see and as long as we are alive, there is that hope.
And my mother was always consoling us.
And we were always talking about what will be after we were liberated.
We were always talking about the B's-- the three B's, things that started with the letter B-- a bath, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] bread and butter.
And there was another B, but I think those were the most important things.
And-- and it's-- I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I always can't say enough about my mother.
It was really her inner strength and her foresight that saw us through.
She is one remarkable lady, as she is today, and not a bitter person, not angry, and very positive.
And she instilled that positive outlook on life to my brother, and particularly to me.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my leg on the train, of course, was in terrible shape.
It was oozing with pus, and lice were crawling all in it and out it.
And it was bloody.
And it was-- it was very bad.
It was a deep hole.
And there was absolutely no-- no medical attention, of course, whatsoever.
As I said, the Russians-- no, I did not say that the Russians then liberated us.
The Germans fled.
And the Russians came onboard the train and led us to a nearby village by the name of Trobitz that was in the area of Leipzig in eastern Germany.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Again, during these 13 days aboard the train, thousands died.
Those who died were-- had to be left along the tracks.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] These were horrible sights.
It's something that I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I just push in the back of my mind.
It just seems as though all of this happened-- just like in another lifetime, as though it really didn't happen to me.
And I think that's one of the reasons that I can talk about it as easily-- not that it is easy to talk about.
It-- it's always-- it never gets better.
I speak frequently about it.
I have spoken since 1979 at schools and organizations.
And it is never easy.
And it's-- it's-- well, it just seems to me as though it's a very bad dream, as though it was a nightmare, as though it happened to somebody else, as though it never really happened to me.
And-- and that's how I rationalize, and that's how I can relate and I can talk about it.
We were liberated by the Russians.
We're forever grateful to the Russians.
I know that we're not always in good terms with that part of the world.
But if it weren't for the Russians, we would definitely not be here today.
In their crude way, they helped us along while we were in this little village.
We took over the houses in Trobitz.
The inhabitants, they left and fled.
We took over their kitchens and their homes.
And we ate of their unfamiliar nourishment-- the foods, the different meats and breads and butter and vegetables and whatnot and everything.
It was a very unfamiliar nourishment for our starved bodies, and certainly didn't agree with us.
We became [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] continuously ill with dysentery.
And with-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we just couldn't tolerate all that unfamiliar nourishment.
Many, many people died in Trobitz, again, from malnutrition and typhoid.
And of course, many people recovered and did very well.
It was my father who had to die, six weeks after we were liberated, of typhoid.
And this was after six years of ridicule and torment and heartache and horror.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] My father died.
And my 12-year-old brother had to help bury him.
[PAUSES FOR 19 SECONDS] [DRINKS] The Russians did the very best that they could for us.
They helped me [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to-- I went daily to a hospital that was set up for us and for the soldiers in the area.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And every day, I was taken to this hospital.
And there, my wound was treated with a yellowish powder, which we later found out was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] penicillin.
And it was a miracle how one could see that every day, the wound would get smaller and smaller.
This hospital-- I was treated-- next-- next to me, there were amputees.
And-- and there were horrendous sights.
But I guess you learn to live with those things.
And I was just very grateful that I responded to medication.
In the interim, our heads were shaved.
And that was the only way that we could rid ourselves of lice.
I know my friends-- I had a very, very dear friend Suzy.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She ran away from the Russians, because she did not want her hair to be shaved.
And I was unable to run.
I wasn't even able to walk.
So the hair was shaved, but I guess that was all of this part of it.
It didn't matter at that point anymore.
I gradually relearned to walk, gradually regained my strength.
My mother, also, was ill with typhoid.
But thank god, she recovered.
And my brother was the only one who really did not take sick, who was physically well.
He was always scrounging and searching for food.
He had still his knapsack.
We all still had our knapsacks.
And his knapsack was always filled with food.
He was always looking for food.
It was only one day after we-- after my father passed away, and after we buried my father, that we were told it was time for us to leave Trobitz.
And-- this thing came off.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we then were transported aboard a truck out of Trobitz.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my mother and I were on board of the truck.
And my brother was nowhere in sight.
Al was nowhere to be found.
And my mother asked to please hold the truck, that he was sure to come.
Surely enough, there he came, running.
Where was he?
He was searching for food.
And he came running.
And we pulled him onto the truck.
And as we pulled him onto the truck, his knapsack fell with all his precious foods.
And there was no way of retrieving that knapsack.
It was a pitiful sight.
And it was-- I mean, this was his security.
And this was-- he felt, I'm sure-- because that's the kind of boy he was and that's the kind of man he is now-- responsible for us.
And he felt that with the knapsack and the food gone, he is not upholding his responsibility for his mother and his sister.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And this is how we left and came to Holland.
Yes.
Just her constant care and her-- her constant-- the hope that she gave us.
And that's all, unfortunately, that she could give us-- it's just constant reassurance.

And I know now how horrendous and how terrible it must have been for a mother to see her children suffer the way she saw us suffer and the way she saw children die, not knowing what would become of us.
And she had nothing else to give us but this inner strength, this-- this will to go on-- that everything will be all right, and we will come out of this.
And it was contagious.
It just reflected right back onto me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was very fortunate to have had my mother with me.
My brother was with my father, and he was with-- with the men.
And he was also two years older than I am.
Therefore, I don't-- for him, it-- it most likely was more difficult than it was for me, because he was older.
I also don't know what it was that he saw and how he perceived what he saw.
It could have been much worse than what I experienced.
And--
